metadata.title: Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality: A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
metadata.journal: Journal for the Scientific Study of Religion

abstract: In lieu of the traditional text data analysis methods, structural topic modeling was utilized to analyze the text contents of 255 self-report inventories of religion and spirituality (R/S) published from 1929 to 2017. The study had two objectives: (a) to clarify and identify the latent dimensions of R/S inherent in the items of the measures; and (b) to examine and demonstrate the usefulness of a longitudinal topic modeling in the study of R/S. We identified 5,617 unique text terms from the measures and fitted topic models on those terms to extract latent dimensions called topics. We also simultaneously analyzed the longitudinal effect of publication decade (i.e., 1950s-2010s) on the topics. A topic model with three topics was chosen to best support the data: Experience of Transcendence (Topic 1), Engagement in Transcendence (Topic 2), and Essence of Transcendence (Topic 3). In addition, the longitudinal analysis revealed that Topic 1 showed a continual increase over the decades, while Topics 2 and 3 both demonstrated a gradual decrease, in effect matching the general trend of Topic 1's increasing popularity in society and the academia.

paragraphs: In the last 50 years, research on or with variables of religion and spirituality (R/S) has consistently expanded to result in at least several thousand research publications (Koenig 2011;Weaver et al. 2006). In effect, even scholars outside of the behavioral, social, and health sciences have increasingly become familiar with and interested in the variables of R/S. However, a recent review in the field of R/S observed this speedy boom in the research of R/S as not only a fruition of "multiplicity and diversity" in theory, methodology, and measures of R/S, but also a realization of the reality of apparent "fragmentation" (Pargament et al. 2013:5).

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: In the midst of this expansion and advancement on R/S, it becomes important to examine then what we have measured so far in the broad domain of R/S, as Kapuscinski and Masters (2010) had stated, "It is time to step back and consider what it is that we are actually measuring" (p. 138). In other words, in order to prevent further fragmentation in the conceptualization of R/S, a reflection is warranted to look back at the way the literature has identified and operationalized R/S. We believe such practice will contribute significantly to both research and practice in the domain of R/S and also bring the R/S field forward without further fragmentation for the following three reasons.

First, many have argued that a lack of integrated effort in identifying the underlying dimensions of R/S resulted in the proliferation of excessive R/S measures, often with construct overlap (Cutting and Walsh 2008;Hill and Edwards 2013;Kapuscinski and Masters 2010). As early as 1984, Gorsuch had criticized the oversupply of self-report R/S inventories with the warning to stop the creation of new measures. Nevertheless, Hill and Edwards (2013) estimated that since the late 1990s, about 100 new self-report instruments related to R/S have been created. Roughly, the total number of R/S measures in the field of psychology of R/S alone is currently estimated to be over 200 and even close to 250. As the plethora of R/S self-report inventories available exists juxtaposed alongside the deficiency of common R/S conceptualizations, we felt that identifying the measured dimensions of R/S would function as the much needed integrated effort in theorizing, defining, or capturing the fundamental constructs of R/S. This would lead to a more cohesive practice of R/S measurement and inventory creation.

Second, one recent review of studies between 1965 and 2009 on the effect of R/S on mental and physical health showed that about two-thirds of research observed a positive association, about 25 percent of the research indicated no association, and about 10 percent a negative association (Koenig 2012). However, in the majority of these studies, the R/S variables were measured with one or two global indexes of R/S, such as church attendance or membership, which impeded the exploration of how, why, or what specific aspects of the R/S variables actually influenced mental or physical health (Hill and Pargament 2008;Koenig 2012). In other words, parsing out the R/S variable would help to clarify the link between specific dimensions/aspects of the R/S variables and the more popular outcome measures such as health (Haber, Jacob, and Spangler 2007) and youth outcomes (Hardy et al. 2019). As a logical progression then, extrapolating the measured dimensions of R/S as measured in the R/S inventories becomes important to further conceptually understand R/S. Third, R/S is described as an intrinsic trait of a human being (Piedmont and Wilkins 2013). If so, understanding the dimensions of R/S would promote further comprehension of the human personality, which has been a domain of study for a long time. Even in the early 20th century, William James argued, "To the psychologist the religious propensities of man must be at least as interesting as any other of the facts pertaining to his mental constitution" (James 2008:10). More recently, one factor analysis research (Piedmont 1999) revealed that the spirituality factor was independent of the basic five dimensions of human personality and that it may potentially be a sixth dimension. Thus, for the above-mentioned three reasons, examining the dimensions of R/S as measured within the proliferating R/S literature was deemed to be an important task for the R/S field as a whole.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: Currently, vast amounts of available definitions exist of R/S in the scholarship without a single consensus. Therefore, we summarized the overall trend of the conceptualizations of R/S in the literature and discussed a few of the more prominent definitions of R/S.

First, there exists a significant overlap in the conceptualization of R/S in general, although the exact relationship between the two constructs still has not been agreed upon (Hill and Pargament 2008;Kapuscinski and Masters 2010;Pargament 2013). The common core shared between R/S seems to relate to "the sacred" (Hill and Pargament 2008;Pargament 2013), or "the transcendent" (de Jager Meezenbroek et al. 2012;Koenig 2011;King and Boyatzis 2015). However, both broader and scholarly audiences often assume that religion is generally more associated with established institutions, doctrines, and traditions, while spirituality is conceptualized as a more individual and personal process (Kapuscinski and Masters 2010;Pargament et al. 2013). However, such simple distinction may be inaccurate and reinforces a false bifurcation between these obviously overlapping constructs.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: JOURNAL FOR THE SCIENTIFIC STUDY OF RELIGION Thus, reflecting on the overlap between the two constructs, one recent conceptualization of R/S in psychology was deemed especially important and relevant to discuss in light of the current study. Pargament et al. (2013) tentatively defined religion as "the search for significance that occurs within the context of established institutions that are designed to facilitate spirituality" (p. 15), and spirituality as "the search for the sacred" (p. 14). The essential characteristics of these definitions are: (a) R/S are closely connected in that the primary goal of religion becomes the promotion of spirituality within established institutions and traditions; (b) both have at the core the sacred that is closely related to the concept of the transcendent. Also, Pargament et al. (2013) stressed that both share the similarities of the dynamic search process, multidimensionality, hierarchical structure, and multivalence. In addition, the authors pointed out two main differences in terms of function and context of R/S: (a) spirituality is pursued to achieve a broader range of significant goals, and (b) religion is rooted in a doctrinally informed institutional context.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
sections.header_norm: dimensions of r s past research
paragraphs: So far, actual past research on the dimensions of R/S have promulgated concrete depictions of the R/S. It also seems that two general approaches have been adopted in the search for the dimensions of R/S: (a) theoretical or conceptual and (b) empirical (Cornwall et al. 1986). These two distinctive approaches, in fact, have developed simultaneously, often informing one another.

The theoretical (i.e., "top-down") approach typically assumed a priori certain dimensions of R/S and operationalized them accordingly. One early example of this approach was delineated in James's (1902James's ( /2008) definition of religion as the "feelings, acts, and experiences of individual men" (p. 49), in which James proposed three components of religion. Later, Allport and Ross (1967) speculated two types of religious motivation as extrinsic and intrinsic.

Alongside this theoretical approach, empirical (i.e., "bottom-up") methods mostly utilized factor analysis on item responses of measures. For instance, Spilka, Armatas, and Nussbaum (1964) analyzed the responses of two groups to 64 items of the God concept, extracting 11 and 12 factors, respectively, for each group; the study served as one of the earlier empirical studies based on factor analysis regarding the dimensions of R/S. More recently, Haber, Jacob, and Spangler (2007) employed a factor analysis on 127 items selected from several R/S measures and extracted seven factors.

Besides these theoretical and empirical approaches, we also located few empirical studies that employed linguistic methods that screened self-report measures of R/S for frequently appearing key terms. Specifically, Kapuscinski and Masters (2010) collected 34 self-report inventories created between 1982 and 2008 in the R/S domain. The authors distinguished four constructs that the measures were purportedly designed to assess by reviewing the key terms defining those constructs: (a) a search for the sacred (referred to as God or transcendent), (b) affective or cognitive components such as feelings, perceptions, awareness, and identity, (c) beliefs or practices associated with a religious institution such as a church or synagogue, and (d) behaviors such as prayer or meditation.

Similarly, Emblen (1992) selected four definitions of religion and 10 definitions of spirituality that were often cited and used in the nursing literature between 1963 and 1989 and identified 48 key terms for religion and 68 for spirituality. For religion, six key terms of person, system, beliefs, organized, practices, and worship loomed most frequently, while for spirituality, nine key terms of person(s), life, principle, animator(s), being, God (god), quality, relationship, and transcendent most often appeared. Based on this analysis of key terms, Emblen (1992) concluded that contrary to the common literature finding of the interrelatedness between R/S, there actually existed vast differences in conceptualizations of the two constructs.

The authors distinguished four constructs that the measures were purportedly designed to assess by reviewing the key terms defining those constructs: (a) a search for the sacred (referred to as God or transcendent), (b) affective or cognitive components such as feelings, perceptions, awareness, and identity, (c) beliefs or practices associated with a religious institution such as a church or synagogue, and (d) behaviors such as prayer or meditation.

Similarly, Emblen (1992) selected four definitions of religion and 10 definitions of spirituality that were often cited and used in the nursing literature between 1963 and 1989 and identified 48 key terms for religion and 68 for spirituality. For religion, six key terms of person, system, beliefs, organized, practices, and worship loomed most frequently, while for spirituality, nine key terms of person(s), life, principle, animator(s), being, God (god), quality, relationship, and transcendent most often appeared. Based on this analysis of key terms, Emblen (1992) concluded that contrary to the common literature finding of the interrelatedness between R/S, there actually existed vast differences in conceptualizations of the two constructs.
sections.preview_head: So far, actual past research on the dimensions of R/S have promulgated concrete depictions of the R/S. It also seems that two general approaches have been adopted in the search for the dimensions of R/S: (a) theoretical or conceptual and (b) empirical (Cornwall et al. 1986). These two distinctive approaches, in fact, have developed simultaneously, often informing one another.

paragraphs: We used structural topic modeling (STM; Roberts, Stewart and Airoldi 2016) to infer the latent dimensions inherent in the multitude of items of the 255 self-report instruments of R/S. STM is an empirical method to find the underlying conceptual/theoretical units of R/S embedded within the verbal texts of the R/S assessment tools. However, there are other popular approaches to text analysis besides STM, which we can be further reviewed in Chen and Wojcik (2016), Grimmer and Stewart (2013), or Iliev, Dehghani, and Sagi (2015).

Traditionally, in social sciences, human coders analyze text data to infer the characteristics and properties intrinsic in the text. In this approach, theory-based categories and coding schemes are first created and coders are trained accordingly. Then, the coders or raters read through the text under analysis with the coding scheme in mind, which functioned like a rake gathering leaves from a forest floor, to deduce targeted categories or constructs. The interrater agreement or interrater reliability of human coding is measured with a coefficient, such as Cohen's kappa (Cohen 1960). Although this method of human coding has been regarded as the gold standard, it loses practicality as the amount of text data increases due to the significant demand for resources like time required for coding (Iliev, Dehghani, and Sagi 2015).

To obviate this unique challenge in human coding, various methods of automated or quantitative text analysis have been developed. Note that many of these new methods also share the same objective of classifying texts into a set of categories as human coding does (Grimmer and Stewart 2013). All of such automated text analysis methods of classification could be divided into two camps depending on whether the classification categories are a priori: (a) known or (b) unknown.

Classification of text data based on known categories consists of two approaches: (a) dictionary method and (b) supervised learning method (Grimmer and Stewart 2013). The dictionary method is similar to the human coding method in that the researchers use predefined set of categories (e.g., happy, joyful, and pleased for Positive Sentiment) to infer the characteristics and features of the texts. In addition, similar to the coding scheme employed in the human coding method, the dictionary method relies on the researcher-defined dictionaries. Despite the similarities, coders are trained prior to coding in the human coding method, but a computer does the job of coding or classification in the dictionary method. Another main difference between the two approaches is that coding or classification of texts can be based on a single word, phrase, sentence, or a larger body of text in the human coding method, but in the dictionary method, the coding is typically based on single words for the computers (Iliev, Dehghani, and Sagi 2015). One well-known example of the dictionary method is Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC; Pennebaker et al. 2015). The dictionary of LIWC is composed of approximately 6,400 words, word stems, and emoticons that are organized into about 90 predefined categories, including 41 categories of psychological constructs, such as affect, cognition, biological processes, and drives as well as a religion category comprising 174 words. Despite the simplicity and flexibility of the dictionary approach, however, a new dictionary needs to be created and validated with each new domain of text, as each new domain of text requires a new coding scheme as in human coding.

Supervised learning method is mainly used in predicting a target feature of text using other characteristics of the text data (Lantz 2015). In supervised learning, the data set with known categories are divided into a training set and a test set prior to model building. The training set is used to build an optimal model in which a combination of features is identified to best predict the target feature. Then, the model is evaluated and validated on the test set. The word supervised reflects the notion that the predicted feature is already known and that the selection of the best predicting characteristics is optimized without any human involvement in the model-building process (Lantz 2015). Two popular supervised learning methods on text data are Naive Bayes and Support Vector Machines and examples of their applications can be found in Lantz (2015) for the former and Joachims (1998) for the latter. One well-known usage of this method is in building a spam-filtering algorithm on e-mails or text messages. Specifically, one can collect thousands of e-mails that have been presorted as spam versus nonspam through human coding, which then helps to predict the known features of each category through other characteristics (i.e., words or phrases) within those e-mails. Next, the researcher can then build a model in which the spam status can be predicted from the words present in the e-mails. For example, it may be that any e-mail containing a word or phrase such as free, extra cash, act now, or Viagra would increase the probability of that e-mail belonging to a spam class. This fitted model of prediction on the training set is then validated and tested further on different data sets.

Unlike the dictionary method and the supervised learning method, in which text data are assigned to predefined classes utilizing the words in the text, the unsupervised learning method, including STM, classifies text data into unknown categories, while simultaneously inferring and discovering those latent categories (i.e., latent themes or topics) based on word co-occurrences (Grimmer and Stewart 2013;Iliev, Dehghani, and Sagi 2015). Due to the estimation of the unknown number of classes or topics, the unsupervised learning method is analogous to principal component analysis (PCA), in which the number of principal components or factors is unknown and thus must be estimated. Topic models are well-known examples of the unsupervised learning method on text data of which Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA; Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003) was the first, widely used topic model that provided a foundation to the ensuing topic models. A precursor of LDA was Latent Semantic Analysis (LSA; Deerwester et al. 1990), another quantitative text-data analysis tool popular in the social sciences, which employs singular value decomposition, a form of eigenvalue analysis. Despite methodological similarity between LSA and LDA (e.g., both conceptualized as a matrix factorization of document-term matrix [DTM]), LSA does not depend on rigorous statistical modeling (Steyvers and Griffiths 2007).

An advantage of unsupervised learning method, like topic models, is then that this method does not need predefined categories (e.g., user-defined dictionaries) generated from human judgment, since the statistical model estimates the categories or topics based on the pattern of word co-occurrences in the text (Iliev, Dehghani, and Sagi 2015). Nevertheless, this crucial advantage comes with a price. Because of the lack of knowledge on the number of hidden classes, the researcher must estimate and determine the number of classes prior to data analysis (so-called enumeration) as is common in PCA. An advantage of topic models, however, is that a piece of text data could belong to multiple latent classes at the same time; the supervised learning method is different in that text data are usually assigned to a single class (e.g., either spam or nonspam exclusively). For this reason, topic models are considered a mixed-membership model (Airoldi et al. 2014) that is more flexible than classical mixture models (McLachlan and Peel 2000) that only allow each document to consist of (or belong to) only one topic. For example, a document (e.g., a measure of R/S) in topic models can be made up of three latent classes called topics, comprising 20 percent of Topic 1, 30 percent of Topic 2, and 50 percent of Topic 3.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: The STM is one of the topic models developed in the interdisciplinary domains of statistics, computer science, machine learning, and computational linguistics. Topic models, a collection of statistical methods of text data analysis, are used to "discover the hidden thematic structure in large archives of documents" (Blei 2012:1). These latent themes in documents are called topics, analogous to factors in factor analysis. STM is built upon LDA (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003), the first and most widely used topic model.

Results of a topic model analysis including LDA and STM typically reveal topics, highprobability words associated with each topic, and topic proportions in each document. The topics are named using the high-probability words in each topic, as factors are named using the contents of the indicator items (analogous to high-probability words) in factor analysis. For example, a topic named Depression can be derived from a group of high-probability terms such as sadness, blue, crying, and hopeless, while a topic name of Substance Abuse can be utilized for a group of terms such as marijuana, alcohol, DUI, and feeling high. In the last few years, the social science research community adopted LDA to analyze psychological text data such as psychotherapy transcripts in order to extract topics from therapeutic conversations (e.g., Imel, Steyvers, and Atkins (2015) analyzed 1,553 psychotherapy and drug therapy transcripts).

Despite its popularity, however, LDA has some weaknesses and STM was designed to compensate for the limitations. First, LDA does not have a framework to incorporate covariates or predictors of topics or topic compositions in documents (Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016).

However, text data are typically collected with metadata that provide unique information about each text, such as author's identity (e.g., name, gender, or nationality) or time of its composition. These metadata may hold significant information about the nature of the text data and, if included in the analysis, could lead to important findings. Second, LDA implicitly assumes independence of topics and thus cannot model direct correlations among topics (Blei and Lafferty 2007). In reality, this assumption of topic independence (analogous to uncorrelated factors from orthogonal rotation in factor analysis) is too restrictive because related topics frequently occur together in documents. STM, on the other hand, overcomes these limitations by allowing covariates into its framework and permitting the correlation of topics (Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016). Thus, STM's statistical method can be thought of as the next step that combines the analytical (i.e., factor analysis) and the linguistic or qualitative study methods of the past. We selected STM because it best fit our study goals over an extensive set of traditional or automated text analysis methods.

sections.preview_tail: In reality, this assumption of topic independence (analogous to uncorrelated factors from orthogonal rotation in factor analysis) is too restrictive because related topics frequently occur together in documents. STM, on the other hand, overcomes these limitations by allowing covariates into its framework and permitting the correlation of topics (Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016). Thus, STM's statistical method can be thought of as the next step that combines the analytical (i.e., factor analysis) and the linguistic or qualitative study methods of the past. We selected STM because it best fit our study goals over an extensive set of traditional or automated text analysis methods.

paragraphs: Topic models assume that text data are organized and grouped in a set of documents called corpus and analyzed at the document level (Grimmer and Stewart 2013). In the current analysis, we considered each measure of R/S as a document, a unit of analysis, and so the corpus of this analysis was made up of 255 documents or R/S measures. Each of these documents, in turn, contained multiple words. Another important assumption of the topic model is the bag of words assumption, in which a text is regarded as a simple collection of words, stripped of the word order and grammatical information between the words (Grimmer and Stewart 2013). Through this assumption, a simple frequency of word occurrence in documents became the focus of our topic modeling research, rather than the word order or the complex relationship between words. Some may wonder how this assumption affects reverse coding or reverse keyed items such as items starting with "I never had an experience . . . " in the Mysticism Scale (Hood 1975). However, we found the assumption matters little in terms of understanding the latent dimensions of the R/S measures because our method still captures the gist of what those items purport to assess (e.g., "experience" in the item above).

Particularly in our analysis, we were interested in the effect of each measure's publication year on the topic prevalence,foot_4 more specifically whether the publication year of each measure was significantly related to the topics (i.e., latent themes) within each measure. Because the covariate in this case was a time-varying one of the publication year and the analysis would yield a longitudinal pattern of topic prevalence, the current analysis was designated as a longitudinal topic model. We hypothesized that the proportion of topics in the collection of 255 R/S measures would vary across the time span of the publication years from 1929 and 2017. For example, given that many of the early R/S measures focused on religion, topics related to religion was predicted to be more prevalent in the early years, while topics related to spirituality was hypothesized to appear more frequently in the later decades.

One of the fundamental questions in studies that involve latent variables (e.g., factor analysis) including topic models, however, is related to the number of latent dimensions in the data because it is not readily observable and needs to be estimated. STM also shares this challenge of estimating the number of topics in documents. Various model diagnostic statistics have been suggested though to ease the challenge. Following the recommendations of the literature (Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016;Taddy 2012;Wallach et al. 2009), we combined four different criteria to decide upon an optimal number of topics in the 255 R/S measures: (a) held-out likelihood (Wallach et al. 2009); (b) semantic coherence (Mimno et al. 2011); (c) exclusivity (Bischof and Airoldi 2012); and (d) residuals (Taddy 2012). Generally, a topic model with an excellent fit would be high on held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, and exclusivity, but low on residuals.

The held-out likelihood represents the "probability of unseen held-out documents given some training documents" (Wallach et al. 2009:2). To calculate the held-out likelihood, the data set (e.g., a set of documents) is first divided into two: one for fitting the model and the other (i.e., held-out) for testing the fit of the model. The former data set is known as the training set and the latter the test set (Hastie, Tibshirani, and Friedman 2009). The held-out likelihood is the loglikelihood calculated from the test set and conceptually analogous to a fit index of a confirmatory factor analysis model on a second data set after the model has been identified with an exploratory factor analysis model on the first data set.

The second criterion of semantic coherence reflects the fact that high-probability terms of a topic tend to occur together across documents under analysis (Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016). Our study used a cohesion score developed by Mimno et al. (2011) to evaluate the optimal number of topics that could provide the most semantically coherent topics. Mimno et al. (2011) showed that this coherence score corresponds well with human judgment of latent themes (i.e., topics) in documents by expert annotators.

The third criterion of exclusivity of topic terms dictates that high-probability terms in one topic should not overlap with high-probability terms in other topics, and that high-probability terms be unique and exclusive to one topic only (Bischof and Airoldi 2012). In general, a topic is interpreted and named based on the high-probability terms in that topic; however, if a topic is to be considered exclusive, the high-probability terms in that topic should have low probability in other topics, thus discriminating one topic from another. If we keep the analogy of topic models to factor analysis and consider topics as factors and terms as factor indicators (usually items of a measure), terms of high exclusivity in topic models are similar to factor indicators that are not cross-loaded onto more than one factor simultaneously. The two criteria of coherence and exclusivity are, in fact, closely related in that a coherent and exclusive topic tends to be semantically more meaningful.

Last, the criterion of residual analysis (Taddy 2012) indicates that given the theoretical multinomial error variance of one in a topic model, a residual variance of larger than one implies a true number of topics larger than the current number of topics. Except for the residuals then, a larger value on the first three above-entioned criteria reflects a better fit.

While acknowledging the usefulness of the four diagnostic indexes, however, we recognized that these criteria simply maximized the model fit without necessarily improving the substantive interpretation of the model (Chang et al. 2009). Thus, along with the four criteria, we carefully examined the substantive, semantic fit of each topic using FREX scores (Roberts, Stewart, and Airoldi 2016), which combine frequency and exclusivity of terms in each topic. Terms that have high FREX scores aid in differentiating topics well.

In all, we fitted 14 topic models (2-to 15-topic models) on the 255 R/S measures. The R statistical environment (R Core Team 2019) was used for data analysis, and the collected text data were first lemmatized (i.e., put into a common form: educated and educating turned into educate) using the TreeTagger software (Schmid 1994) and the koRpus package (Michalke 2016) in R. Next, the "tm" package (Feinerer and Hornik 2015) preprocessed (e.g., removed punctuations, numbers, and common function words) the lemmatized text documents and also created a DTM as an input for the analysis. The DTM, as well as the time covariate of publication year, was then used in the "stm" package (Roberts, Stewart, and Tingley 2016) to fit the structural topic model.

paragraphs: We included in the DTM not only single-word terms called unigrams, but also two-and threeword phrases called bigrams (e.g., Jesus Christ) and trigrams (e.g., religious (and/or) spiritual belief), which were created using the "RWeka" package (Hornik, Buchta, and Zeileis 2009) after lemmatization. The total number of terms, including bigrams and trigrams was 79,597, which included 4,108 unigrams, 32,188 bigrams, and 43,271 trigrams. We treated the unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams as the same unit of analysis and simply called them terms.

Of the 79,567 terms, about 73,950 of them occurred only once or twice across the entire 255 R/S measures. Thus, such sporadically appearing terms were excluded from further data analysis in order to simplify the process and to aid in the convergence of the model. For the final data analysis then, only 5,617 terms remained. As can be seen from the descriptive statistics of terms in Table 1, about half of the 5,617 terms were bigrams; the unigrams made up about one-third of the terms and appeared most often with a mean frequency of 23.79. Among the unigrams, god appeared 1,453 times followed by life (1,157 times), feel (737 times), and religious (645). The frequencies (i.e., the number of times a term occurred among the 255 documents) of uni-, bi-, and trigrams ranged from 3 to 1,453 (M = 11.56, SD = 40.15).

As mentioned before, the publication years of the 255 R/S measures functioned as the predictor of topic proportions, ranging from 1929 to 2017. In categorizing by the publication decades, there was one measure for each decade of 1920s and 1930s, three for 1940s, eight for 1950s, 21 for 1960s, 28 for 1970s, 41 for 1980s, 50 for 1990s, 80 for 2000s, and 22 for 2010s. Because only 13 measures had been published up to 1950s, we combined the relatively small number into one category of 1950s. a This number represents the number of instances or tokens of words, not the frequencies of each token. For example, Jesus Christ is one of the 2,813 bigrams and its frequency (the number of times it occurred) throughout the 255 measures was 63.

Figure 1 Four diagnostic indexes of structural topic models with varying numbers of topics: 2-15 topics According to the R package "stm" manual (Roberts et al. 2017), 3-10 topics are usually deemed appropriate for a short corpus (e.g., around a few hundred documents) with a clear and definite subject matter. We, therefore, fitted 2-15 structural topic models on the preprocessed DTM with a covariate of decades predicting the longitudinal change of each topic's prevalence at any given time. The fitted models were then initialized with a spectral algorithm (Arora et al. 2013) and the values of the diagnostic indexes are presented in Figure 1.

The values of the four diagnostic fit indexes, however, did not indicate unanimous support regarding the number of topics. Exclusivity and residuals preferred a large number of topics; however, held-out likelihood and semantic coherence favored a smaller number of topics. Given the lack of cutoffs for these diagnostic fit indexes, we then examined the top 20 FREX scored terms of each topic in each model to determine which model had substantive support. First, the two topics in the two-topic model did not seem to measure focused latent themes but were

We included in the DTM not only single-word terms called unigrams, but also two-and threeword phrases called bigrams (e.g., Jesus Christ) and trigrams (e.g., religious (and/or) spiritual belief), which were created using the "RWeka" package (Hornik, Buchta, and Zeileis 2009) after lemmatization. The total number of terms, including bigrams and trigrams was 79,597, which included 4,108 unigrams, 32,188 bigrams, and 43,271 trigrams. We treated the unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams as the same unit of analysis and simply called them terms.

Of the 79,567 terms, about 73,950 of them occurred only once or twice across the entire 255 R/S measures. Thus, such sporadically appearing terms were excluded from further data analysis in order to simplify the process and to aid in the convergence of the model. For the final data analysis then, only 5,617 terms remained. As can be seen from the descriptive statistics of terms in Table 1, about half of the 5,617 terms were bigrams; the unigrams made up about one-third of the terms and appeared most often with a mean frequency of 23.79. Among the unigrams, god appeared 1,453 times followed by life (1,157 times), feel (737 times), and religious (645). The frequencies (i.e., the number of times a term occurred among the 255 documents) of uni-, bi-, and trigrams ranged from 3 to 1,453 (M = 11.56, SD = 40.15).

As mentioned before, the publication years of the 255 R/S measures functioned as the predictor of topic proportions, ranging from 1929 to 2017. In categorizing by the publication decades, there was one measure for each decade of 1920s and 1930s, three for 1940s, eight for 1950s, 21 for 1960s, 28 for 1970s, 41 for 1980s, 50 for 1990s, 80 for 2000s, and 22 for 2010s. Because only 13 measures had been published up to 1950s, we combined the relatively small number into one category of 1950s. a This number represents the number of instances or tokens of words, not the frequencies of each token. For example, Jesus Christ is one of the 2,813 bigrams and its frequency (the number of times it occurred) throughout the 255 measures was 63.

Figure 1 Four diagnostic indexes of structural topic models with varying numbers of topics: 2-15 topics According to the R package "stm" manual (Roberts et al. 2017), 3-10 topics are usually deemed appropriate for a short corpus (e.g., around a few hundred documents) with a clear and definite subject matter. We, therefore, fitted 2-15 structural topic models on the preprocessed DTM with a covariate of decades predicting the longitudinal change of each topic's prevalence at any given time. The fitted models were then initialized with a spectral algorithm (Arora et al. 2013) and the values of the diagnostic indexes are presented in Figure 1.

Exclusivity and residuals preferred a large number of topics; however, held-out likelihood and semantic coherence favored a smaller number of topics. paragraphs: We named Topic 1 Experience of Transcendence based on the common theme of the top 20 terms. For one thing, transcendent occurred three times in the top 20 list. As noted before, transcendence lies at the core of spirituality and is also central to religiosity (Emmons 2005;King, Clardy, and Ramos 2014;Pargament et al. 2013). In effect, the terms related to Topic 1 convey different modalities of experiencing transcendence. Terms such as connect, consciousness, experience feel, and spiritual belief all allude to different ways of apprehending transcendence through thoughts, feelings, and perceptions regardless of whether the experience is within a theistic religious tradition or a more humanistic phenomenological understanding. Consequently, we use the term Experience of Transcendence to convey the ways in which people can encounter transcendence.

We note that the topic also had terms such as wilderness, never experience, and illness that did not at first intuitively relate to the latent theme of Experience of Transcendence. Extensive review of the less intuitive terms in their specific contexts, however, evidenced that such terms stemmed from contexts in which people experience transcendence. In fact, wilderness appeared only in the measure, Wilderness Spirituality (Bloom 2008), with a total frequency of 32 and the term was observed to connect the unique role of the wilderness in promoting transcendence (e.g., feeling awe or peace; being connected to a higher power in wilderness). The phrase never experience appeared 31 times across five R/S measures mostly within two mysticism measures, including the Mysticism Scale (Hood 1975), and those items with this phrase (i.e., reverse keyed items) actually assessed spiritual experiences of feeling awe, peace, wonder, happiness, an altered sense of reality, or feeling connected with the sacred or the transcendent. In addition, the term illness appeared in 14 R/S measures and was always discussed in relation to transcendence, focusing on the catalyst role of illness as an existential crisis in one's religious and spiritual life (e.g., in relationship to the transcendent). Thus, combining the most frequently appearing terms and their relevance to modalities of or contexts connected to experiencing transcendence, we determined that Topic 1 represented Experience of Transcendence.

On the other hand, the name Engagement in Transcendence best fit the latent theme of Topic 2. At first, the central theme seemed less clear by only reading the top 20 terms of the second topic. We, therefore, examined four R/S measures that had the most number of terms assigned to Topic 2, such as the Committed Consensual Measures (CCM; Allen and Spilka 1967), and specifically surveyed the context surrounding the top 20 terms. It then became evident that these terms came about in contexts representing notions of participating in and responding to transcendence. The most prevalent themes involved engagement in religious groups or entities conveyed by terms such as church member, local religious, local religious group, religious denomination, and church often and also in ways people may respond to transcendence: loving, teaching, dealing, try hard (to) carry (my religion over into all my other dealings in life), answer many questions. These terms are indicative of fidelity, which suggests a devotion and commitment to transcendent ideals (King, Clardy, and Ramos 2014). Terms such as behind (my) whole approach (to life), especially important, and important answer also convey an integration of beliefs into one's life orientation that is evident in both the engagement noted by the more affiliative terms (e.g., church member) and in the actions that are connected to religious and spiritual ideals (e.g., loving, try hard carry). Although we considered naming Topic 2 Responding to Transcendence, we chose Engagement in Transcendence to designate the reciprocity between the individual and his/her understanding of transcendence. Existing measures seem to capture activities that may be a response to experiencing transcendence and/or may further deepen one's experience of transcendence. For example, attending a religious group may be a response to experiencing the love of God and/or it may promote one's knowing of God more fully.

Although three of the top 20 terms had the word church, as well as local religious group and religious denomination, all of which reflected religion, we emphasize that the terms related to Topic 2 can be relevant to both spirituality and religion defined earlier. Many of the Topic 2 terms focused on the goals and/or the processes central to the search for significance, which is a goal of both R/S such as dealing(s) (in) life, question meaning (of) life, and (spiritual beliefs lie) behind (my) whole approach (to life) (Fenzel 2002) with omitted contents in the parentheses. We, therefore, deemed Engagement in Transcendence to be the best name for Topic 2.

Last, Topic 3 received the name Essence of Transcendence based on the top 20 terms and the 10 most representative R/S measures of the Topic 3. Whereas Topic 1 included terms related to different people's experiences of transcendence and Topic 2 identified terms involving people's response to or engagement with transcendence, Topic 3 terms describe the nature or the object of transcendence. Topic 3 highlights social scientists' tendency to assess people's understanding of the nature of transcendence, and up until more recently this has been concentrated on Judeo-Christian conceptualizations of God. Eight of the top 20 terms included god (God), which never occurred in the top 20 terms for either Topic 1 or 2, and thus discriminated Topic 3 well. We then examined the context of the use of god (God) and it appeared mostly in relation to Judeo-Christianity or the Bible. God, in fact, represented a monotheistic but patient supernatural being, who could do miracles but also punish human beings. Although most of the top 20 terms and their respective R/S measures dealt with Judeo-Christianity, an important exception of allah (Allah) surfaced with the second highest FREX score within the top 20 terms list. The word allah (Allah), as a matter of fact, occurred a total of 50 times across seven R/S measures and most frequently in Psychological Measure of Islamic Religiousness (24 times; Abu Raiya et al. 2008) and Multidimensional Measure of Islamic Spirituality (16 times; Dasti and Sitwat 2014).

In these measures, Allah consistently reflected a monotheistic God as the absolute being around whom a Muslim aligns her/his religious and spiritual life. Thus, because of Topic 3's coverage of the monotheistic being of both Judeo-Christianity and Islam, we also considered naming Topic 3 Monotheism. However, we decided against the name because we recognized the changing landscape of religiosity and spirituality in the 21st century. We hoped to identify labels for the topics that would endure despite the increasing diversity of spiritual and religious affiliation, beliefs, and practices (Pew Research Center 2017) and also remain relevant with the continued proliferation of R/S measures that address more diverse and nuanced forms of transcendence (see Ai, Harris, and Wick in press;King et al. 2017). The fact that only four of the 225 measures included in our study targeted the evaluation of Islam R/S and that they have come about fairly recently since 2007 are indicative of the increasing variations of conceptualizations and traditions of transcendence that are being measured. Thus in the end, we labeled Topic 3 Essence of Transcendence to allow for beliefs about transcendence that are not limited to theism, but may include a continuum of self-other distinction that ranges from complete distinction between the self and the transcendent other to crossing of boundaries between self and transcendent other to more immanent forms of transcendence that involve notions of ontological unity between self and other.

paragraphs: To analyze the topics in greater detail, we next calculated topic proportion for each R/S measure. For example, the Wilderness Spirituality (Bloom 2008) measure was made up of 99.6 percent of Topic 1 and less than 1 percent of Topics 2 and 3, while CCM and the Literal, Antiliteral, and Mythological (LAM; Hunt 1972) scales were comprised of 99.5 percent of Topic 2 and 99.8 percent of Topic 3, respectively. The mean topic proportions overall in the 255 measures were 36.3 percent (Topic 1), 13.4 percent (Topic 2), and 50.3 percent (Topic 3), which meant that in general 36.3 percent (13.4 percent or 50.3 percent) of the items of each R/S measure would manifest the latent theme of Experience of Transcendence (Engagement in or Essence of Transcendence, respectively).

We then investigated the intertopic relationships or the codependence of the topics. Topic proportions in each document are a type of compositional data because the topic proportional sum in each document is always 1 percent or 100 percent. This restriction in compositional data, however, has been known to introduce spuriousness in Pearson's product-moment correlation coefficient (Pawlowsky-Glahn, Egozcue, and Tolosana-Delgado 2015). To deal with this inherent issue then, we utilized a measure of correlation between two topics such that the correlation is proportional to the variance of log ratios between the two topics, as suggested by van den Boogaart and Tolosana-Delgado (2013). These alternative correlation indicators among the three topics, in fact, came out very small and close to zero (i.e., r < .0001), demonstrating the independence of the three topics. The correlation indicators also revealed that the pattern of combination or mixing of the topics in each measure did not evidence any specific pattern but rather widely varied across different R/S questionnaires.

Topic proportions in each document are a type of compositional data because the topic proportional sum in each document is always 1 percent or 100 percent. This restriction in compositional data, however, has been known to introduce spuriousness in Pearson's product-moment correlation coefficient (Pawlowsky-Glahn, Egozcue, and Tolosana-Delgado 2015). To deal with this inherent issue then, we utilized a measure of correlation between two topics such that the correlation is proportional to the variance of log ratios between the two topics, as suggested by van den Boogaart and Tolosana-Delgado (2013). These alternative correlation indicators among the three topics, in fact, came out very small and close to zero (i.e., r < .0001), demonstrating the independence of the three topics. The correlation indicators also revealed that the pattern of combination or mixing of the topics in each measure did not evidence any specific pattern but rather widely varied across different R/S questionnaires.

For example, the Wilderness Spirituality (Bloom 2008) measure was made up of 99.6 percent of Topic 1 and less than 1 percent of Topics 2 and 3, while CCM and the Literal, Antiliteral, and Mythological (LAM; Hunt 1972) scales were comprised of 99.5 percent of Topic 2 and 99.8 percent of Topic 3, respectively. The mean topic proportions overall in the 255 measures were 36.3 percent (Topic 1), 13.4 percent (Topic 2), and 50.3 percent (Topic 3), which meant that in general 36.3 percent (13.4 percent or 50.3 percent) of the items of each R/S measure would manifest the latent theme of Experience of Transcendence (Engagement in or Essence of Transcendence, respectively).

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: This research examined the latent themes called topics in the items of 255 R/S measures published from 1929 to 2017. Psychologists typically employ factor analysis to examine latent dimensions of items in psychological measures as shown in the seminal research of Gorsuch (1984). If this study had utilized factor analysis to explore the latent dimensions in the thousands of items of the 255 R/S measures (although we believe factor analysis is methodologically and conceptually distinct from STM), it would have required tens of thousands of participants to answer all of the items for a consistent estimation of parameters. For practical reasons in terms of time, effort, and cost, we applied STM and analyzed the texts of the items directly, focusing on the pattern of term co-occurrences in the R/S measures themselves. Because of STM's flexibility that allows the modeling of the effect of covariates on the proportion of topics in documents, we were able to inspect the effect of publication years on the estimated topic proportions.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: We identified three topics of Experience of Transcendence (Topic 1), Engagement in Transcendence (Topic 2), and Essence of Transcendence (Topic 3). We want to emphasize that the core themes of Topics 1, 2, and 3 all conceptually encompassed the definitions of both R/S, which were reviewed in the introduction of this research. Much of the R/S literature indicates significant conceptual commonality between R/S (Hill and Pargament 2008;Kapuscinski and Masters 2010;Pargament et al. 2013), and the 20 top FREX score terms for Topics 1, 2, and 3 are consistent with this overlap. Our findings, along with other studies point to transcendence as a common element between spirituality and religion that serves to unify these distinct but overlapping domains. Although the essence of transcendence may be experienced and engaged in differently in various spiritual and religious contexts, the STM results emphasized the centrality of reckoning with transcendence across spirituality and religion.

These findings reflect the fact that social scientists assess people's encounter with transcendence, their participation in and response to transcendence, and their understanding of the nature of transcendence as important elements of R/S. It is noteworthy that none of our three topics is exclusive to either spirituality or religion. Both Topics 1 and 2 reflect specific ways people encounter, engage in, and search for the sacred or for significance within the context of spirituality and/or religion (Pargament et al. 2013). Topic 3 reflects how researchers measure people's understanding of transcendence. Some of the high-frequency terms for Topic 1, Experience of Transcendence, included spiritual, transcendent, feel, connect, experience, insight, wilderness, and life find. Those for Topic 2 were church, dealing life, join, local religious group, question meaning life, and religious denomination. Those for Topic 3 were christian, allah, miracle, god will, god god, punish, client, and god feel. Although some of the terms may seem more stereotypically related to spirituality (e.g., connect, question meaning life) and some to religion (e.g., local religious group, god), when viewed through the lens of transcendence, it becomes apparent that all terms are potentially related to both spirituality and religion. No terms are exclusive. For even local religious groups can be contexts for engaging the transcendent. Not all persons share the same beliefs about transcendence, nor do they employ the same modalities or behaviors for experiencing or responding to transcendence. Rather, this analysis of existing R/S measures points to the prominence and complexity of transcendence within social scientists' assessment of spirituality and religion.

sections.header_norm: topics 1 and 2 experience of and engagement in transcendence
paragraphs: Furthermore, there was a close match between our findings in topic modeling and the two studies relying on human judgment and categorization of R/S measures, demonstrating the usefulness and validity of topic modeling in studies of R/S. Notably, we compared Topics 1 and 2 of the current research with the core concepts of R/S identified in previous research that utilized a similar method of item content analysis. However, it is important to note that these past studies, such as Emblen (1992) and Kapuscinski and Masters (2010), did not utilize automated methodology nor based the analysis on a statistical model like ours.

First, the four core concepts of spirituality as delineated in Kapuscinski and Masters (2010) appeared to encompass Topics 1 and 2 of this study. More specifically, Topic 1, Experience of Transcendence, resembled a combination of the first two elements of spirituality of Kapuscinski and Masters: (a) a search for the sacred and (b) affective or cognitive components as evidenced in the top terms of Topic 1, such as spiritual, transcendent, feel, experience, and insight. Second, Topic 2, Engagement in Transcendence, paralleled the third and fourth elements of Kapuscinski and Masters: (c) institutional beliefs or practices (e.g., in church or synagogue) and (d) behaviors (e.g., prayer). Topic 2 of this research indeed pointed to various institutions, such as church and denomination as well as actual behaviors such as loving, teaching, and praying and indicated the contents of prayers, such as question(ing) meaning (of) life and dealing(s) (in) life.

Next, in comparing the key words of spirituality and religion as identified in Emblen (1992) with the 20 top terms of Topics 1 and 2 in our research, we found both close similarity, but also an important contrast between the conclusions of Emblen and our results. For spirituality according to Emblen and Topic 1 Experience of Transcendence, the terms of person, life, transcendent, and relationship (or connect) were shared. Nevertheless, for religion according to Emblen and our Topic 2 Engagement in Transcendence, no specific key terms actually aligned; however, the key terms for religion in Emblen such as system, beliefs, organized, practices, and worship seemed comparable to the top terms of Topic 2 that reflected a response to or participation in transcendence. Alongside the close resemblance between Emblen's key words and our Topics 1 and 2 terms, we also agree with Emblen's conclusion that despite the seeming interrelatedness of R/S, the two concepts need to be defined and consequently understood differently. However, we reached the same conclusion for different reasons. Emblen's analysis resulted in separate categories of R/S, whereas our STM highlighted that there may be a reciprocal dynamic between an individual and his or her understanding of transcendence that is common within R/S, but that the patterns of thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that make up that reciprocal dynamic may be observably distinct within different R/S contexts.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: Unlike Topics 1 and 2 that evolved around processes related to how one experiences or engages in his or her understanding of the transcendent, Topic 3, Essence of Transcendence seemed to relate to one's understanding of the nature of transcendence. The term named god or allah refers to the transcendent to which one can relate to, and the terms also describe the potential attributes of the transcendent-patient, (performs) miracle. It seemed then that Judeo-Christianity of the Western society and culture, to a greater or lesser extent, influenced the pattern of expression of spirituality and religion in the majority of the 255 R/S measures. Of course, the nature of transcendence would manifest differently in different cultures and societies with varying ontological beliefs (e.g., Buddhist, Hindu, or humanistic contexts).

Prominence of Topic 3 in this research resonates with a remark of Hill and Edwards (2013) that the majority of R/S measures were developed within Judeo-Christian contexts and thus that they lack cultural sensitivity. The authors went even further to say that those R/S measures developed in the United States were based mostly on European American Protestantism and that they do not cover the diverse religious experiences of different racial/ethnic groups even within the United States. The 255 R/S measures of this study included some recently developed measures outside the Judeo-Christian tradition, such as several measures of Islamic beliefs and practices and some measures of Buddhist and Hindu R/S, such as the Buddhist Coping Scale (Phillips et al., 2009) and Hindu Religious Coping Scale (Tarakeshwar, Pargament, and Mahoney 2003). However, the number of non-Judeo-Christian measures was remarkably small compared to those developed within the context of Judeo-Christianity. In all, we agree with Hill and Edwards (2013) regarding the necessity of more culturally sensitive R/S measures that can appropriately assess individuals from not only diverse R/S backgrounds, but also of different race/ethnicity, age, socioeconomic status, and education.

The 255 R/S measures of this study included some recently developed measures outside the Judeo-Christian tradition, such as several measures of Islamic beliefs and practices and some measures of Buddhist and Hindu R/S, such as the Buddhist Coping Scale (Phillips et al., 2009) and Hindu Religious Coping Scale (Tarakeshwar, Pargament, and Mahoney 2003). However, the number of non-Judeo-Christian measures was remarkably small compared to those developed within the context of Judeo-Christianity. In all, we agree with Hill and Edwards (2013) regarding the necessity of more culturally sensitive R/S measures that can appropriately assess individuals from not only diverse R/S backgrounds, but also of different race/ethnicity, age, socioeconomic status, and education.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: The longitudinal trend of these topics demonstrated informative patterns (Figure 2) of increasing Topic 1 and dwindling Topics 2 and 3 over a time period from the 1920s to 2010s. This overall trend suggests that those R/S measurements focused on Experience of Transcendence has been on the rise while those measurements assessing Engagement with and Essence of Transcendence on the decline. We want to connect this trend of changing topic proportions in the R/S measures with the overall trend of the general U.S. society. For example, Wuthnow (1998:3) argues that growing number of Americans is now involved in "a new spirituality of seeking . . . they increasingly negotiate among competing glimpses of the sacred, seeking partial knowledge and practical wisdom." More recently Pew Research Center (2017) has documented the continuation of this trend. In other words, there seems to be an apparent longitudinal pattern in which people in the United States want to experience the transcendent (Topic 1) more than to engage in or understand the nature of the transcendent (as in an institutional religious context; Topics 2 and 3). Furthermore, the authors took notice that the popularity of Topic 1 as a research topic among psychologists increased markedly along the same time trajectory, resulting in increased proliferation of scale development in Topic 1. This increasing popularity of Topic 1 seems to be an ongoing trend even in the present (Hardy and King 2019;Pargament et al. 2013).

paragraphs: This study had two important limitations. First, it seems that there is no clear consensus or guideline regarding the diagnostic criteria and their cutoffs for model selection in topic modeling, in particular regarding the number of topics. One of the critical issues in latent variable modeling is identifying the number of latent dimensions in data, such as the number of factors in factor analysis, the number of classes in mixture modeling, and the number of topics in topic modeling. Several model fit indexes have been introduced and used in topic modeling, but there still exists no consensus regarding model fit indexes and their specific cutoff values as definite decision points. The current study adopted four criteria of held-out likelihood, semantic coherence, exclusivity, and residuals for the number of topics best supported by the data. However, due to the lack of agreement on the cutoffs on these criteria, we only consulted them as rough guidelines and relied more on substantive fit.

There are other methods available than these four criteria that are used to aid the decisionmaking process in choosing the number of topics. We became curious what these methods could tell us about our data and tried four popular methods that are widely used in topic models including LSA and LDA. However, note that our final model of choice like in many STM studies was estimated with a covariate and that at this point we do not fully understand the magnitude of the longitudinal covariate's effect on the number of topics in the current research. Keeping this in mind, we applied four methods to our text data only (i.e., excluding the covariate): (a) Profile Log-likelihood (PL; Zhu and Ghodsi 2006) based on the singular values of the DTM, (b) PL based on the eigenvalues of the DTM, (c) a scree plot based on the eigenvalues of the DTM (Cattell 1996), and (d) Hierarchical Dirichlet Process (HDP; Teh et al. 2006).

The PL method outputs are depicted in Figures 3A and 3B , the location of the scree is important for the determination of the number of topics, but it is not clearly distinguishable in the plot; however, our best judgment points to somewhere between five to eight topics. However, Figure D, an output from an HDP analysis, supports 13 (according the mode), or 15 or 16 topics (according to the mean of 15.52, represented by a broken red line). HDP is a nonparametric topic modeling method; unlike LDA or STM in which the researcher should specify the number of topics prior to analysis, HDP estimates the best number of topics from the data directly. The output in Figure D is from 50,000 Gibbs sampling iterations (the first 1,000 burn-in iterations were removed from each 2,000 iterations that was repeated 50 times with random starts). We used a C++ program developed by Wang and Blei (2010) for this HDP analysis with vague concentration priors specified in Teh et al. (2006Teh et al. ( :1575)).

After applying these four different procedures and examining the varied outputs on the number of topics (ranging from one to 16), we realized the challenge of selecting the best number of the topics from our text data. However, these results are from analyzing the text data only excluding the covariate. Our structural topic model, on the other hand, was estimated with a covariate, although the potential effect of including that on the estimation of topic numbers is not yet fully known. Also, these methods did not consider the substantive fit of topics and their constituent terms.

Second, we analyzed bigrams and trigrams in addition to single-word terms. Some of these two-and three-word phrases occurred frequently and were informative. For example, Jesus Christ Note: A (B): Profile likelihood based on the singular values (eigenvalues) of the document-term matrix. (C) Scree plot based on the eigenvalues of the document-term matrix. (D) Number of topics estimated from a Hierarchical Dirichlet Process analysis (50,000 Gibbs samples).

and attend religious service were two of the most frequently appearing bigrams and trigrams. However, the bigrams and trigrams resulted after removing the stop words from the text and thus, some of them did not necessarily make sense by themselves alone. In fact, one needs to guess the original form and meaning by surmising the deleted stop words. For example, in the top 20 terms of Topic 2, Engagement in Transcendence, dealing(s) (in) life and question meaning (of) life were not very difficult to guess the original, intact phrases, although one still needs to figure out the removed stop words. Another rather challenging example was god god in Topic 3, which occurred 39 times across 31 R/S measures. We carefully examined the texts and found out that this bigram was mostly from a concatenation of god at the end of a sentence and another one at the beginning of a sentence. Another rare instance of this bigram was from God (of very) God in the Religious Belief Inventory (Lee 1965). We decided to keep this rather counterintuitive bigram mainly because it still captured the core theme of Topic 3, God. In the future, the effect of including or excluding this type of bigrams will need to be further studied.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: First, any method that is limited to considering social science measures of a phenomenon is precluded from drawing conclusions on the actual experience or the nature of the phenomena. In regards to our study, we analyzed existing R/S measures between 1927 and 2017. Consequently, the scope of our commentary is confined to quantitative measurement. That said, we can point to sociological survey that suggest that traditional forms of religious participation that have informed the assessment of R/S are shifting (Pew Research Center 2017). Detecting such trends is important and points to the need for more nuanced measures that can elucidate different conceptualizations and experiences of transcendence that may result in distinct and observable patterns of behavior.

Second, other methodologies and disciplines offer direction for effective measurement development. For example, within developmental psychology, both quantitative and qualitative studies have been designed to excavate the psychological processes within religious and spiritual development to further understand both the nature and function of R/S in young people's lives (for review, see Hardy and King 2019;King and Boyatzis 2015). Complex models and qualitative forms of research provide more nuanced insight into the varieties of transcendent beliefs and experience (for review, see Hardy et al. 2019). For example, in a study of diverse spiritual exemplars around the world, some youth did not believe in a theistic form of God, but described their spiritual pursuits in the context of finding meaning and direction in life through their connection to humanity, nature, or a sense of absolute truth. Even in one case, an agnostic Jewish male talked about the spiritual salience of his sense of connection to all the Covenant People of God (King, Clardy, and Ramos 2014). New emphasis on measurement invariance also allows for the development of R/S measures that can be modified for different R/S contexts and therefore be inclusive of different worldviews and conceptualizations of transcendence. For example, the Measure of Diverse Adolescent Spirituality (MDAS) was developed with the intention of being modified to assess for different conceptions of transcendence but measures one's experience of transcendence, the integration of transcendent beliefs, and the behavioral responses to it (see King et al. 2017).

Last, the study of R/S is always informed by theology and philosophy. As Hill and Edwards (2013) commented, the North American Protestant context in which much R/S research is conducted has influenced how measures have been designed. The gap between the key terms revealed in our STM and the diversifiying R/S beliefs, affiliations, and practices reported by surveys such as those conducted by the Pew Research Center (2017), suggest that social scientists need to be more aware of how theology, philosophy, and ontological assumptions inform the manner in which they develop R/S assessment tools. Diverse theological and philosophical traditions must inform measurement construction if social scientists are going to effectively assess and understand diverse personal experience of transcendence. Many spiritual and religious traditions have thousands of years of spiritual wisdom that when integrated with psychological theory provides ample rich direction for further research on transcendence. Only further research will tell if the opinions like those of Gorsuch (1984) that suggested that R/S is best conceptualized and operationalized as a single concept is correct. Our guess is that with the growing sophistication of knowledge of psychology and R/S wisdom along with methodological advances, the field will witness a continued differentiation of R/S conceptualizations and assessments that will hopefully further our understanding of the complex role of transcendence in human life.

The gap between the key terms revealed in our STM and the diversifiying R/S beliefs, affiliations, and practices reported by surveys such as those conducted by the Pew Research Center (2017), suggest that social scientists need to be more aware of how theology, philosophy, and ontological assumptions inform the manner in which they develop R/S assessment tools. Diverse theological and philosophical traditions must inform measurement construction if social scientists are going to effectively assess and understand diverse personal experience of transcendence. Many spiritual and religious traditions have thousands of years of spiritual wisdom that when integrated with psychological theory provides ample rich direction for further research on transcendence. Only further research will tell if the opinions like those of Gorsuch (1984) that suggested that R/S is best conceptualized and operationalized as a single concept is correct. Our guess is that with the growing sophistication of knowledge of psychology and R/S wisdom along with methodological advances, the field will witness a continued differentiation of R/S conceptualizations and assessments that will hopefully further our understanding of the complex role of transcendence in human life.

sections.paper_id: Kim et al. - 2020 - Dimensions of Religion and Spirituality A Longitudinal Topic Modeling Approach
paragraphs: We employed STM, a relatively new approach in text data analysis, to identify the latent dimensions of R/S as apparent within currently existing R/S inventories and extracted three topics of Experience of, Engagement in, and Essence of Transcendence. These three topics illuminated the latent dimensions of R/S as studied in the research literature and pointed to the usefulness and validity of the topic modeling method within the social sciences. Topic models, as a method of automated text analysis, recently gained much popularity among researchers in the social sciences, including within psychology in which it was used to analyze counseling transcripts and texts in social networks (e.g., Twitter and Facebook). Thus, it is our hope that the researchers in R/S see potential benefits of topic modeling and utilize and perceive this method as a viable method of text data analysis in a manner that continues to build the field of the scientific study of R/S.
